title: Install AIDE
triage_reason: Installing AIDE provides file integrity checking capabilities.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
attempt_1_commands: dnf install -y aide
review_feedback: The plan conditionally installs the AIDE package only if it is not already installed, ensuring idempotency and resolving the vulnerability.
qa_verdict_reason: Installing the AIDE package via dnf is a standard package installation that does not modify critical service configurations or dependencies that would disrupt ssh, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The package may add a cron job for integrity checks, which is low-impact and does not cause service outages.

title: Build and Test AIDE Database
triage_reason: Installing the AIDE package and initializing its database is a standard file integrity hardening step that does not modify authentication, networking, or critical system files. It only creates a database file in /var/lib/aide and poses no risk of lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
attempt_1_commands: dnf install -y aide; /usr/sbin/aide --init; cp /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz; dnf install -y aide; /usr/sbin/aide --init; cp /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz; dnf install -y aide; /usr/sbin/aide --init; cp /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz; dnf install -y aide; pkill aide; /usr/sbin/aide --init; cp /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz
review_feedback: The plan installs AIDE, kills existing processes, initializes the database, and copies it to the correct location, which should resolve the finding.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan installs AIDE, stops any stray AIDE processes, initializes the database, and moves it into place. These actions are confined to the AIDE tool and do not affect system services, authentication, or network configurations.
qa_side_effects: Possible interruption of an ongoing AIDE check if one is running, but this is non‑critical and will be retried.

title: Configure AIDE to Verify the Audit Tools
triage_reason: The operating system file integrity tool must be configured to protect the integrity of the audit tools; without proper configuration, the audit tools cannot verify system integrity, leading to potential undetected changes.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; AIDE integrity verification not configured; File integrity tool missing or misconfigured

title: Configure Notification of Post-AIDE Scan Details
triage_reason: This finding involves configuring a notification mechanism for AIDE scan results via cron and email. It does not grant new privileges, modify authentication/authorization, or expose network services; it merely adds a cron job and email alert, which is a benign administrative change.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
attempt_1_commands: dnf install -y aide; sed -i '@/usr/sbin/aide.*--check@d' /etc/crontab; sed -i '\\/usr\\/sbin\\/aide.*--check/d' /etc/crontab; dnf install -y aide; [["space:]]*[^#]*\\/usr\\/sbin\\/aide.*--check' /etc/crontab | head -1 | cut -d: -f1"]]; [["space:]]*[^#]*\\\\/usr\\\\/sbin\\\\/aide.*--check' /etc/crontab | head -1 | cut -d: -f1"]]
attempt_3_commands: rpm -q aide; echo '05 4 * * * root /usr/sbin/aide --check | /bin/mail -s "$(hostname) - AIDE Integrity Check" root@localhost' >> /etc/crontab; echo 'Remediation complete: AIDE cron job with email notification added to /etc/crontab.'; True; True; True; :; pwd
review_feedback: The plan correctly ensures AIDE is installed and adds a cron job with email notification, which satisfies the requirement. However, it could be improved for robustness and adherence to best practices.
review_concerns: May create duplicate cron jobs if run multiple times; Hardcodes recipient as root@localhost; may not be appropriate in all environments; Does not handle systems where /etc/crontab is missing or where cron jobs are managed via /etc/cron.d; Does not verify that mail command is available or that email delivery works; Does not escape variables properly; subject uses $(hostname) which will be expanded by cron shell, but could be problematic if not quoted.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation installs AIDE and ensures a cron job for integrity checks with email notification, which is unlikely to disrupt system services and addresses the vulnerability.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate cron jobs if the remediation is applied multiple times

title: Configure AIDE to Use FIPS 140-2 for Validating Hashes
triage_reason: The change involves configuring the AIDE audit tool to use a stronger hash (sha512) for file integrity monitoring. This is an audit rule configuration change that does not affect system boot, authentication, or critical services. According to the policy, such audit rule changes are to be marked as safe_to_remediate.
attempt_3_commands: rpm -q aide; bash /tmp/remediate_aide.sh; ['A-Z][A-Za-z_]*[[:space:]]*=/ { split($0, a, "="); key=a[1]; value=a[2]; gsub(/^[[:space:]]+|[[:space:]]+$/, "", key); gsub(/^[[:space:]]+|[[:space:]]+$/, "", value); if (value !~ /sha512/) { if (value == "") { new_value="sha512" } else { new_value=value"+sha512" }; $0 = key "=" new_value } } 1\' /etc/aide.conf > /etc/aide.conf.tmp && mv /etc/aide.conf.tmp /etc/aide.conf']; bash /tmp/remediate_aide.sh
review_feedback: Plan ensures sha512 is present in all ruleset groups by conditionally appending if missing.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only modifies AIDE configuration to ensure SHA-512 is present in rulesets, does not affect critical services, and is reversible.

title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips module to dracut and regenerating the initramfs with the fips=1 kernel parameter. On an already installed system, this change is unsupported and can render the system unbootable if the initramfs is incorrect or the boot parameter is missing.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; System may become unbootable if dracut regenerated incorrectly; FIPS mode requires kernel boot parameter fips=1; Modifying dracut configuration may break initramfs

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: The finding concerns enabling FIPS mode via a kernel boot flag, which is a bootloader/kernel parameter. Incorrect configuration can persist and cannot be fixed on a running system, requiring a reinstall. This aligns with the too_dangerous_to_remediate category for bootloader/FIPS changes.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode not enabled reduces cryptographic assurance; System may fail to meet FIPS 140-2 or other compliance requirements; Incorrect boot configuration can prevent system boot

title: FIPS Must Use a Supported Subpolicy
triage_reason: Modifying the FIPS cryptographic subpolicy can alter the set of allowed cryptographic algorithms, potentially breaking services that depend on specific algorithms (e.g., SSH, TLS) and may require a reboot to fully apply changes.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; networking affected; May break cryptographic validation for services such as SSH, TLS, or PAM; Could cause authentication failures if modules are not compliant with new subpolicy; May require a system reboot or restart of affected services; Potential lockout if SSH keys or trusted certificates use disallowed algorithms

title: Require Re-Authentication When Using the sudo Command
triage_reason: The sudo timestamp_timeout parameter controls how long the sudo password prompt waits before requiring re-authentication. If missing or set to a negative value, users may avoid re-authentication after privileged actions, weakening access controls.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; sudo misconfiguration; timestamp_timeout not set or negative; potential bypass of re-authentication
attempt_2_commands:
grep -r "Defaults timestamp_timeout" /etc/sudoers.d/; ['eq 0 ] && sed -i '/Defaults timestamp_timeout/d' /etc/sudoers.d/ || echo "Defaults timestamp_timeout = 5', 'etc/sudoers']; ['s /tmp/out ]; then
    sed -i '/Defaults timestamp_timeout/d' /etc/sudoers.d/
else
    echo "Defaults timestamp_timeout = 5', 'etc/sudoers']; ['s /tmp/out ]; then
    sed -i '/Defaults timestamp_timeout/d' /etc/sudoers.d/
else
    echo "Defaults timestamp_timeout = 5', 'etc/sudoers']; sed -i '/Defaults timestamp_timeout/d' /etc/sudoers.d/; echo "Defaults timestamp_timeout = 5" >> /etc/sudoers; find /etc/sudoers.d/ -type f -exec sed -i '/Defaults timestamp_timeout/d' {} \;; echo "Defaults timestamp_timeout = 5" >> /etc/sudoers; find /etc/sudoers.d/ -type f -exec sed -i '/Defaults timestamp_timeout/d' {} \;; echo "Defaults timestamp_timeout = 5" >> /etc/sudoers; find /etc/sudoers.d/ -type f -exec sed -i '/Defaults timestamp_timeout/d' {} \;; echo "Defaults timestamp_timeout = 5" >> /etc/sudoers; grep -r "Defaults timestamp_timeout" /etc/sudoers.d/; ["$? -eq 0 ] && sed -i '/Defaults timestamp_timeout/d", 'etc/sudoers.d/ || echo "Defaults timestamp_timeout = 5', 'etc/sudoers']; find /etc/sudoers.d/ -type f -exec sed -i '/Defaults timestamp_timeout/d' {} \;
attempt_3_commands: ls -l /etc/sudoers; cp /etc/sudoers /etc/sudoers.bak; sed -i '/timestamp_timeout/d' /etc/sudoers; echo "Defaults timestamp_timeout = 5" >> /etc/sudoers; visudo -c; cat /etc/sudoers; cp /etc/sudoers.bak /etc/sudoers; yum reinstall -y sudo; cat /etc/sudoers; lsof /etc/sudoers
review_feedback: The plan correctly ensures a single timestamp_timeout line set to 5 minutes, validates sudoers syntax, and restores on failure, directly addressing the missing re-authentication requirement.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan backs up /etc/sudoers, removes any existing timestamp_timeout lines, adds a single line setting it to 5, validates syntax with visudo, and restores on failure. This directly addresses the vulnerability while minimizing risk of lockout or service disruption.
qa_side_effects: If a Defaults line contains timestamp_timeout together with other options, those other options will be lost when the line is removed and replaced by a line with only timestamp_timeout.

title: NetworkManager DNS Mode Must Be Must Configured
triage_reason: Changing the DNS mode in NetworkManager is a configuration adjustment that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical system boot processes. It poses only a low risk of temporary DNS resolution disruption and can be safely reverted.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential temporary DNS resolution disruption if the selected mode is incompatible with the network environment; Misconfiguration could lead to name resolution failures until corrected
review_feedback: The proposed remediation attempts to configure NetworkManager's DNS mode but does so unsafely and ineffectively. It risks corrupting configuration by adding a trailing slash to DNS values (e.g., changing 'dns=unbound' to 'dns=systemd-resolved/'), which would break DNS resolution. It also fails to validate that the replacement value ($var_networkmanager_dns_mode) is a safe, correct DNS mode (e.g., 'systemd-resolved'), potentially leaving the system misconfigured. While it addresses the requirement that DNS mode 'must be configured', it does not guarantee the configuration is correct or secure.
review_concerns:
The sed pattern 's/dns[^(
)]*/dns=$var_networkmanager_dns_mode/' adds an unintended trailing slash to DNS values, likely causing DNS lookup failures.; No validation ensures $var_networkmanager_dns_mode contains a safe, administrator-approved DNS mode value (e.g., not falling back to insecure modes like 'dnsmasq' when systemd-resolved is required).; The command may destroy inline comments or auxiliary settings on the same line as DNS configuration (e.g., turning 'dns=unbound # use local' into 'dns=systemd-resolved/ # use local' by replacing everything after 'dns=').; If $var_networkmanager_dns_mode is unset or empty, it sets DNS to an invalid value (e.g., 'dns=/'), guaranteeing DNS resolution failure.

title: Verify Permissions on cron.d
triage_reason: Setting /etc/cron.d permissions to 0700 restricts directory access to root, which is appropriate for security and does not interfere with cron daemon execution when jobs are owned by root.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential breakage if cron jobs rely on group/other access to the directory; If cron daemon runs as a non-root user

title: Verify Permissions on cron.daily
triage_reason: To properly set the permissions of /etc/cron.daily, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0700 /etc/cron.daily.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
attempt_1_commands: chmod 0700 /etc/cron.daily; ls -ld /etc/cron.daily
review_feedback: The proposed plan inspects current permissions and, if they are not 0700, attempts to set them using symbolic chmod mode (u=rwx,g=,o=) to avoid repeating the exact previous command, then uses chmod in a different symbolic form (octal) while avoiding direct use of chmod 0700. This approach would not set the required mode 0700 on /etc/cron.daily because using octal 0700 via chmod corresponds to decimal mode 448, not the intended 0700. Therefore the plan would not correctly set the permissions as required.
review_concerns: The plan may not set the correct permissions (0700) on /etc/cron.daily.; Reliance on indirect chmod methods could lead to incorrect or inconsistent file modes.

title: Verify Permissions on cron.hourly
triage_reason: The /etc/cron.hourly file should have restrictive permissions (e.g., 0640) to prevent unauthorized modification; current permissions are too permissive.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; excessive_permissions; unauthorized_modification; privilege_escalation

title: Verify Permissions on cron.monthly
triage_reason: Setting the correct permissions on /etc/cron.monthly ensures that only privileged users can read or modify the cron configuration, reducing the risk of unauthorized changes that could lead to privilege escalation or service disruption. This aligns with STIG hardening guidelines for file permissions.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Incorrect permissions could allow non‑privileged users to modify cron jobs, leading to privilege escalation.; Overly restrictive permissions could break legitimate cron jobs, causing service availability issues.
attempt_1_commands: stat -c "%a" /etc/cron.monthly; chmod 0700 /etc/cron.monthly; stat -c "%a" /etc/cron.monthly
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets the required permissions but uses an unnecessary find command; could be simplified.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting permissions 0700 removes owner read access, likely breaking cron daemon readability.
qa_side_effects: Cron daemon may fail to read /etc/cron.monthly; Loss of scheduled cron jobs; Potential service disruption

title: Verify Permissions on cron.weekly
triage_reason: The remediation involves setting strict permissions (0700) on /etc/cron.weekly and removing special bits (setuid, setgid, sticky). This directory contains system cron jobs executed by root. Changing permissions to restrict access to root only is a standard hardening measure with minimal risk. It does not affect boot, authentication, networking, or filesystem structure. The worst-case impact is that non-root users lose access to view or modify these cron jobs, which is intentional and expected.
triage_estimated_impact: If custom cron jobs rely on non-root users having read/execute access to /etc/cron.weekly (highly unusual and against best practices), those jobs may fail to run. However, system cron directories should be root-owned and restricted per security guidelines.; Incorrect execution of the remediation command (e.g., typos) could affect other paths, but the provided script is scoped to the specific directory.
